questions.question: According to the Coalition Agreement of February 2026, what is the planned breakdown between spending cuts and new revenue to achieve a balanced budget by 2029?
options: 50% spending cuts and 50% new revenue
Spending cuts and new revenue in equal proportions
Spending cuts with a much larger share than new revenue
New revenue with a much larger share than spending cuts
questions.explanation: The Coalition Agreement specifies a trajectory of approximately EUR 1.2 billion in savings split 80% spending cuts and 20% new revenue, reflecting a heavy emphasis on reducing expenses rather than increasing taxes.
questions.sourceTitle: Regional budget: targeting balance by 2029

questions.question: What major structural reform is planned in the Brussels regional administration as part of the 2026 budget measures?
options: Reduction of the number of regional civil servants through mandatory layoffs
Merger of 25 regional structures into 4 entities with expected operational cost reductions
Complete privatization of regional public services
Transfer of all administrative functions to the municipalities
questions.explanation: The Coalition Agreement includes an administrative reform that consolidates 25 regional structures into 4 entities (called pillars) with an expected 20 to 30% reduction in operational costs, distinct from the hiring freeze which explicitly excludes layoffs.
questions.sourceTitle: Regional budget: targeting balance by 2029

questions.question: According to the Regional Policy Declaration of February 2026, which new institution was created to address public cleanliness through citizen input?
options: A deliberative committee combining randomly selected citizens and elected representatives
A regional enforcement task force composed exclusively of municipal police officers
An independent audit body reporting directly to the European Commission
A private consulting firm contracted to design waste management policies
questions.explanation: The Brussels Parliament voted to create a 7th deliberative committee on public cleanliness, bringing together 45 randomly selected citizens and 15 MPs to formulate recommendations on waste management. This represents a new governance approach combining democratic representation with citizen participation.
questions.sourceTitle: Cleanliness: regional brigades and immediate fine collection

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism through which producers of packaging and tobacco products will fund Brussels cleanliness initiatives?
options: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) contributions under the EU single-use plastics directive
A mandatory municipal tax levied on all retail businesses selling packaged goods
Voluntary corporate sponsorship agreements negotiated annually with the Region
A dedicated surcharge added to consumer purchases at checkout registers
questions.explanation: The Brussels Parliament approved an ordinance transposing the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) agreement, requiring producers to contribute funding. The EPR framework, based on the EU's single-use plastics directive, ensures that producers of covered products finance cleanliness efforts rather than taxpayers bearing the full cost.
questions.sourceTitle: Cleanliness: regional brigades and immediate fine collection

questions.question: Under the February 2026 Coalition Agreement, what replaced the Renolution energy renovation program?
options: A mandatory energy audit requirement for all buildings
Zero-interest loans for energy renovation projects
Direct subsidies distributed by the City of Brussels
Tax credits for renewable energy installation
questions.explanation: The agreement replaced Renolution with a zero-interest loan scheme worth EUR 200 million. However, the absence of a dedicated energy transition budget alongside this change has raised questions about the Region's climate commitments.
questions.sourceTitle: Climate: LEZ maintained, Renolution replaced, targets pending

questions.question: What legal action did the Brussels government take in March 2026 regarding green zones?
options: It implemented new regulations to expand protected green areas
It filed an appeal against a construction freeze ruling from October 2025
It designated new brownfield sites for environmental protection
It merged environmental oversight with the water management authority
questions.explanation: The government appealed the October 2025 ruling that had suspended all new construction in Brussels' green zones, citing the need for legal clarity. This move drew criticism from environmental associations and created tension with the coalition agreement's brownfield protection commitments.
questions.sourceTitle: Climate: LEZ maintained, Renolution replaced, targets pending

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the 'Only once' principle mentioned in Brussels's digital policy?
options: To limit citizens to one online login across all regional services
To prevent citizens from submitting duplicate administrative requests
To stop asking citizens for data that the administration already holds
To ensure each citizen interacts with only one government agency
questions.explanation: The 'Only once' principle is part of administrative simplification efforts and aims to reduce bureaucratic burden by preventing redundant data collection. This mechanism is designed to make interactions with government more efficient and user-friendly.
questions.sourceTitle: Digital: bridging the digital divide and administrative simplification

questions.question: Which institution operates as the regional IT centre and technical operator for smart city strategy in Brussels?
options: Easy Brussels
Paradigm (CIRB)
IRISbox
BeCentral
questions.explanation: Paradigm, also known as CIRB, serves as the IT centre for the Brussels-Capital Region and is the technical operator responsible for implementing the smart city strategy. Understanding this institutional structure is key to tracking how digital governance commitments are actually implemented.
questions.sourceTitle: Digital: bridging the digital divide and administrative simplification

questions.question: To address the construction crisis, the Brussels regional government plans to create an Urban Free Zone with three main support mechanisms. Which of the following is NOT one of these three levers?
options: Reduction of property tax and registration rights within the zone
A single Fast-Track administrative window for businesses
Direct subsidies for construction material purchases
A dedicated employment cell through Actiris for jobs in the zone
questions.explanation: The three planned levers are the tax lever (reduced property tax and registration rights), the administrative lever (Fast-Track window), and the human lever (dedicated Actiris employment cell). Direct subsidies for materials are not mentioned as a planned mechanism in the Regional Policy Declaration.
questions.sourceTitle: Economy: Urban Free Zone (Port + Audi Forest), AI capital

questions.question: Which federal measure, implemented in March 2026, directly impacts Brussels's hotel sector and tourism competitiveness?
options: Reduction of VAT on hotel stays to encourage tourism
Increase of VAT on hotel stays from one rate to another
Elimination of all taxes on overnight accommodations
Introduction of a new licensing fee for hotel operators
questions.explanation: The federal government raised VAT on hotel stays effective 1 March 2026, a measure that significantly affects Brussels since the region accounts for the majority of the country's tourist and business overnight stays. The regional government aims to partly offset this increase through free zones and other attractiveness measures.
questions.sourceTitle: Economy: Urban Free Zone (Port + Audi Forest), AI capital

questions.question: Which institution will take over full management of Dutch-speaking out-of-school care in Brussels starting September 2026?
options: The Flemish Community (VGC)
The French Community (FWB)
The Brussels Region
Opgroeien
questions.explanation: The BOA decree transition is a major governance shift in Brussels. From September 2026, the VGC will assume complete responsibility for policy, accreditation, budgets and coordination of Dutch-language out-of-school care, replacing the old quality labeling system with a new VGC framework.
questions.sourceTitle: Education: childcare places, school dropout and Cocof/VGC tensions

questions.question: In Brussels education governance, what is the primary role of Cocof and VGC?
options: They directly operate all schools in Brussels
They exercise transferred competences in education from the Communities for French-speaking and Dutch-speaking education respectively
They only provide vocational training to adults
They coordinate European education policy
questions.explanation: Cocof and VGC are community-level bodies that handle education competences within Brussels: Cocof manages French-speaking education matters like continuing education and lifelong learning, while VGC oversees Dutch-speaking education, culture and welfare. This split reflects Brussels' bilingual nature and the federal structure of Belgium.
questions.sourceTitle: Education: childcare places, school dropout and Cocof/VGC tensions

questions.question: What is the main institutional response to address language barriers for jobseekers in Brussels?
options: Language assessment upon registration with mandatory French/Dutch training if skills are absent
Automatic deportation of non-speakers to other regions
Voluntary language courses offered only to unemployed women
Replacement of Dutch with English in all employment services
questions.explanation: The Regional Policy Declaration includes mandatory language assessment at registration and compulsory French/Dutch training for jobseekers lacking these skills. This directly addresses bilingualism as a lever for employment access in Brussels.
questions.sourceTitle: Employment: 70% target, enhanced activation and bilingualism

questions.question: Which specific geographic areas in Brussels will receive dedicated employment support through a specialized Actiris unit?
options: The Port of Brussels and the Audi Forest site, targeting industrial and logistics occupations
All 19 Brussels municipalities equally
Only the Brussels city centre and surrounding wealthy districts
The airport and highway rest areas
questions.explanation: The DPR establishes a dedicated Actiris unit for the Urban Free Zones (Port of Brussels + Audi Forest site) to provide targeted support for industrial and logistics occupations. This represents a geographically focused intervention strategy.
questions.sourceTitle: Employment: 70% target, enhanced activation and bilingualism

questions.question: What is replacing the Renolution premium system for home renovations in Brussels from 2027 onwards?
options: Increased subsidies managed by Urban.Brussels
Zero-interest loans with an annual envelope until 2029
Mandatory renovation grants from the European Union
Tax credits for homeowners who improve energy performance
questions.explanation: The coalition agreement of February 2026 explicitly replaces Renolution premiums with zero-interest loans starting in 2027, with a EUR 200 million envelope until 2029. This shift aims to support renovations while remaining budget-neutral for the Region, though housing experts have raised concerns about accessibility for lower-income households.
questions.sourceTitle: Housing: EUR 400M for the SLRB, 1,000 public housing units

questions.question: What is the target energy performance standard that all Brussels homes must achieve by 2033?
options: EPC label A or B for all properties
EPC label C as a minimum requirement
EPC label E at minimum
EPC label D for residential buildings
questions.explanation: The content states that all Brussels homes must reach EPC label E by 2033 to comply with regional requirements. This benchmark is significant given that currently 32% of homes on sale carry the worst labels (F or G), meaning substantial renovation efforts are needed across the housing stock.
questions.sourceTitle: Housing: EUR 400M for the SLRB, 1,000 public housing units

questions.question: In the Brussels-Capital Region's institutional reform, how many main organizational pillars will the 25 merged structures be consolidated into?
options: 2 pillars
3 pillars
4 pillars
6 pillars
questions.explanation: The title explicitly states that 25 structures are being merged into 4 pillars as part of the region's institutional reform. This consolidation is a key structural change in Brussels governance designed to improve efficiency and coordination.
questions.sourceTitle: Institutional reform: merging 25 structures into 4 pillars

questions.question: According to the Bilingualism Masterplan, what type of plan must each Brussels hospital now submit to address language services?
options: A multilingual recruitment strategy
A language policy plan
A staff rotation schedule
A patient feedback system
questions.explanation: The Bilingualism Masterplan requires each Brussels hospital to submit a 'language policy plan' (taalbeleidsplan) to guarantee access to care in both official languages. This is part of a cross-cutting institutional commitment to strengthen bilingualism across public services.
questions.sourceTitle: Institutional reform: merging 25 structures into 4 pillars

questions.question: Under the new coalition agreement of February 2026, what will replace the Metro 3 project as originally planned?
options: A bus rapid transit system connecting to the airport
A tram line completing the loop of the existing network
An extension of the pre-metro to the northern districts
A new cycling superhighway across the region
questions.explanation: The agreement freezes Metro 3 for 10 years and redirects investment toward tramway development, specifically Tram 15 and a tram completing the existing network loop. This represents a fundamental shift in Brussels' long-term mobility strategy.
questions.sourceTitle: Mobility: new regional plan, Metro 3 frozen, LEZ maintained

questions.question: According to the coalition agreement, what is the guiding hierarchy principle for Brussels' future mobility policy?
options: Cars first, then public transport, cyclists, and pedestrians
Equal priority for all transport modes to ensure fairness
Pedestrians, then cyclists, then public transport, then cars
Public transport first to maximize ridership efficiency
questions.explanation: The STOP principle establishes a clear priority order: pedestrians > cyclists > public transport > cars. This principle will guide all future mobility decisions and represents a major reorientation away from car-centric planning toward sustainable urban mobility.
questions.sourceTitle: Mobility: new regional plan, Metro 3 frozen, LEZ maintained

questions.question: What is the Regional Security Council (CORES) and who participates in it?
options: A council comprising the 19 Brussels mayors, 6 police zone chiefs, the Brussels King's Prosecutor and OCAM representatives, convened to address major security threats
A federal body based in Antwerp that oversees all Belgian police operations
A private security company contracted to protect government buildings
An EU institution responsible for coordinating security across all member states
questions.explanation: CORES was activated by the minister-president on 4 March 2026 following international events, bringing together local and regional security actors. This multi-level coordination structure illustrates Brussels governance's approach to crisis management.
questions.sourceTitle: Security: anti-drug commissioner, €10M for stations, stable crime rate

questions.question: According to the safe.brussels report, how has Brussels' overall crime rate changed over the past decade?
options: It has declined in per-capita terms, despite an increase in recorded offences
It has remained completely stable with no changes whatsoever
It has exploded dramatically, making Brussels significantly more dangerous
It has fluctuated wildly with no discernible trend
questions.explanation: While the absolute number of recorded offences has remained stable, the per-capita crime rate has declined thanks to population growth. However, specific offence categories (drugs, firearms, sexual violence) show sharp increases, which justifies the Regional Policy Declaration's targeted interventions.
questions.sourceTitle: Security: anti-drug commissioner, €10M for stations, stable crime rate

questions.question: According to the 2026 Coalition Agreement, what new dedicated regional function was created to address drug-related issues in Brussels?
options: A regional drug commissioner
A ministry of addiction prevention
A federal narcotics task force
A community drug treatment board
questions.explanation: The coalition agreement specifically announces the creation of a dedicated regional drug commissioner function as part of cross-cutting social and security measures. This represents a new institutional responsibility within Brussels governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Social affairs: EUR 10M for stations, drug commissioner, services protected

questions.question: What approach does the Democratic Regional Platform (DPR) adopt to address disability inclusion across Brussels policies?
options: Creating separate disability-focused government departments
Limiting disability support to physical accessibility standards (PMR)
Integrating disability considerations across all regional policies through 'handistreaming'
Delegating disability matters entirely to federal authorities
questions.explanation: The DPR introduces 'handistreaming,' which integrates the disability dimension across all regional policies, going beyond just physical accessibility measures. This mainstreaming approach ensures disability considerations inform decisions across all government sectors.
questions.sourceTitle: Social affairs: EUR 10M for stations, drug commissioner, services protected

questions.question: What is the main objective of the CRMS reform in Brussels urban planning?
options: To eliminate all heritage protection in Brussels to speed up construction
To unblock renovation projects in protected areas by changing the Royal Commission's role from binding to advisory
To increase the number of monuments classified as protected heritage sites
To transfer heritage protection decisions from regional to federal authorities
questions.explanation: The CRMS reform replaces the binding opinion of the Royal Commission for Monuments and Sites with a simple advisory opinion, aiming to facilitate renovation projects while still maintaining heritage protection. This is a key structural reform outlined in the Regional Policy Declaration.
questions.sourceTitle: Urban Planning: faster permits and planning amnesty

questions.question: Which institution approved the Jubelpark renovation project as part of a federal cooperation mechanism?
options: The Brussels Parliament
The Brussels Regional Government alone
The federal Council of Ministers through Beliris
The Royal Commission for Monuments and Sites
questions.explanation: Beliris is a federal cooperation mechanism for funding infrastructure in Brussels. Although the Jubelpark falls under federal competence, the renovation works directly affect Brussels territory and are managed through this coordination structure to prepare the park for Belgium's bicentenary in 2030.
questions.sourceTitle: Urban Planning: faster permits and planning amnesty

questions.question: Under Brussels law, which government institution is legally required to integrate disability considerations into all regional policies, including public procurement?
options: The Brussels-Capital Region government, as mandated by the 2016 handistreaming ordinance
Only the transport authority (STIB), according to European accessibility standards
The federal Belgian government, through the Directorate General for Persons with Disabilities
Individual municipalities, as established by the Regional Policy Declaration of 2026
questions.explanation: The handistreaming ordinance of 8 December 2016 requires the Brussels-Capital Region to systematically incorporate disability into all regional policies. This legal framework is distinct from European standards and is a regional Brussels requirement.
questions.sourceTitle: Accessibility and disability in Brussels: transport, employment and data

questions.question: What is the main philosophical shift that occurred in the ACS scheme following the 2015 reform?
options: From structural support for the non-profit sector to an activation policy focused on moving unemployed people into sustainable employment
From regional employment support to federal government control
From subsidising non-profits to exclusively supporting local authorities
From requiring job experience to eliminating all diploma requirements
questions.explanation: The 2015 reform marked a fundamental change in ACS's purpose—moving away from simply funding permanent positions in non-profits toward using these positions as stepping stones for unemployed jobseekers to find sustainable work. This shift has created tensions, as non-profits argue their roles (home care, childcare) are permanent necessities, not temporary training grounds.
questions.sourceTitle: Subsidised Contract Workers: the weakened pillar of Brussels non-profit employment

questions.question: What is the 'ACS differential' problem that weakens non-profit employers?
options: The regional premium fails to fully cover the salary and benefits costs that employers must offer ACS workers, forcing organisations to absorb the difference
ACS workers receive lower salaries than regular staff, creating unfair working conditions
The Brussels-Capital Region provides different subsidy levels to different non-profit sectors
ACS positions are only available to workers with prior sector experience
questions.explanation: Non-profit employers must pay ACS workers the same salaries and conditions as regular staff due to sectoral agreements, but the regional premium doesn't cover these full costs. This gap is especially damaging for smaller, subsidy-dependent organisations. The content identifies this structural underfunding as a core problem for the sector's sustainability.
questions.sourceTitle: Subsidised Contract Workers: the weakened pillar of Brussels non-profit employment

questions.question: How are citizens selected to participate in Brussels Parliament's deliberative commissions?
options: Through a voluntary application process open to all Brussels residents
Through a double random draw using national register numbers from the democratie.brussels platform
By nomination from local municipality councils
Through a competitive interview process conducted by parliamentary staff
questions.explanation: The Brussels Parliament uses a transparent random selection mechanism to ensure representative participation. Citizens aged 16 and over are randomly selected from the national register, with the process overseen by the democratie.brussels platform to guarantee transparency and fairness.
questions.sourceTitle: Citizens' assemblies: 6 commissions completed, 205+ recommendations, 7th in preparation

questions.question: According to research on Brussels residents' well-being, which of the following is identified as the MOST influential factor affecting life satisfaction?
options: Suitable housing and accommodation
Physical and mental health
Social relationships and community activities
Employment and work opportunities
questions.explanation: The IBSA Focus No. 77 study identifies health (physical and mental) as the most influential determinant of life satisfaction, followed by standard of living, social relationships, housing, and work. This finding comes from analysis of EU-SILC 2023 survey data and helps explain what drives well-being in the Brussels region.
questions.sourceTitle: Well-being and quality of life in Brussels: indicators, rankings and challenges

questions.question: According to the Regional Policy Declaration (DPR) of February 2026, how are data centres addressed in Brussels governance?
options: Data centres are explicitly mentioned as a priority in the digital transition strategy
Data centres are not specifically referenced, with digital transition and energy-climate strategy addressed separately
Data centres are integrated into a comprehensive energy-digital policy framework
Data centres are managed exclusively at the federal level with no regional involvement
questions.explanation: The content reveals a governance gap: Brussels' Regional Policy Declaration contains no specific reference to data centres, despite their growing energy impact. This shows how rapidly emerging challenges like AI-driven data centre demand can outpace policy frameworks.
questions.sourceTitle: Data centres and AI: impact on the Brussels energy system

questions.question: What significant shift has occurred in how artificial intelligence consumes energy within data centres?
options: Training AI models now accounts for the majority of energy consumption
Inference—the everyday use of AI models by millions of users—has become the dominant energy consumer, surpassing model training
Energy consumption is equally split between training and inference activities
Data centre energy consumption for AI has remained stable since 2024
questions.explanation: A crucial shift is underway: inference (user queries) now drives most AI energy consumption rather than training. This means the everyday operation of services like ChatGPT strains electricity systems more than building the models themselves—a key insight for understanding future energy demands.
questions.sourceTitle: Data centres and AI: impact on the Brussels energy system

questions.question: Which institutions have direct control over compulsory education in Brussels?
options: The Brussels-Capital Region government alone
The Wallonia-Brussels Federation (for francophone schools) and the Flemish Community (for Dutch-language schools)
The Belgian federal government in coordination with Brussels authorities
Local Brussels municipalities in partnership with the Brussels Region
questions.explanation: The content explains that Brussels residents pay regional taxes to the Brussels-Capital Region, yet it has virtually no power over compulsory education. Instead, the Wallonia-Brussels Federation manages francophone schools while the Flemish Community manages Dutch-language schools—creating what the text calls an 'institutional lasagna' where the Brussels government cannot prevent budget cuts decided by these external bodies.
questions.sourceTitle: Compulsory education in Brussels: the impact of FWB and Flemish Community budget cuts

questions.question: What is the main structural problem that prevents the Brussels government from addressing education budget cuts?
options: The Brussels Region lacks sufficient tax revenue to fund schools independently
Education is controlled by the Communities (FWB and Flemish Community) rather than by the Brussels-Capital Region, over which Brussels has no direct leverage
The Brussels Parliament has rejected proposals to take control of education funding
International law prevents regional governments from managing compulsory education
questions.explanation: The dossier identifies a fundamental governance problem: Brussels residents pay regional taxes to the Brussels-Capital Region, yet compulsory education is managed by the FWB (francophone) and Flemish Community (Dutch-language), whose budgets and cuts are decided by their own parliaments. This means the Brussels government cannot prevent or influence education decisions affecting Brussels families—a key issue for understanding Brussels governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Compulsory education in Brussels: the impact of FWB and Flemish Community budget cuts

questions.question: Which Brussels institution offers free assessment and referral services for businesses facing financial difficulties?
options: Centre pour Entreprises en difficulté (CEd), operated by hub.brussels
Revival by Pulse Foundation
Urban Free Zone administrative office
Regional Court of Commerce
questions.explanation: The CEd provides free assessment and referral services as a first-line support mechanism for businesses in difficulty. This service is one of several regional mechanisms available in Brussels to help prevent or manage business closures.
questions.sourceTitle: Bankruptcies in Brussels: overview, sectors and regional mechanisms

questions.question: What constitutional mechanism was triggered when a conflict of interest motion was filed against the police zone merger project in the Brussels Parliament on 13 March 2026?
options: A 60-day consultation period between the Brussels Parliament and the federal Chamber
An immediate suspension of the federal government's executive powers
A mandatory referendum among Brussels citizens on the merger
A review by the Court of Justice of the European Union
questions.explanation: The conflict of interest motion triggered a 60-day consultation period, which suspends the federal legislative procedure and forces inter-institutional dialogue, though it does not definitively block the project. This is a key procedural safeguard in Belgium's system of governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Police zone merger: from 6 to 1, vote expected before summer 2026

questions.question: According to the government agreement of February 2026, what is a key difference between the new Regional Mobility Plan and the previous Good Move plan?
options: The new plan introduces circulation cells for the first time in Brussels
The new plan focuses smaller cells around school zones rather than entire neighbourhoods
The new plan eliminates all traffic calming measures across the region
The new plan removes the requirement for public transport improvements
questions.explanation: The agreement specifically states that the new plan will have smaller cells centred on school zones, representing a shift in scale and focus compared to Good Move's neighbourhood-wide approach. This change aims to maintain traffic calming while addressing concerns about the original plan's scope.
questions.sourceTitle: Good Move: new regional plan to succeed it

questions.question: What major infrastructure issue became a focal point of dispute between a municipality and the regional government regarding Good Move implementation?
options: A cycling path was built without consulting local residents in advance
Public transport funding was redirected away from the affected commune
A street in Jette remained closed to traffic beyond the planned trial period, with residents and the municipality seeking its reopening
A new bollard installation project was approved without municipal consent
questions.explanation: Avenue Broustin in Jette exemplifies the tensions between regional and local governance: closed since 2021-2022 as part of a Good Move neighbourhood cell, the municipality has formally demanded its reopening and threatens legal action, highlighting implementation challenges that the new plan must address through better municipal coordination.
questions.sourceTitle: Good Move: new regional plan to succeed it

questions.question: Under the February 2026 government agreement, what are the two main changes to how Brussels LEZ fines work?
options: Annual pass option for non-compliant vehicles and a lower per-infraction fine amount
Stricter enforcement starting immediately and removal of all tolerance periods
Increase in fines combined with mandatory vehicle scrapping programs
Extension of the LEZ to surrounding regions and mandatory emission testing
questions.explanation: The agreement introduced an annual pass system (at 350 EUR or 200 EUR social rate) allowing non-compliant vehicle owners to drive legally, and reduced the per-infraction fine from 350 EUR to 80 EUR. These reforms aim to reduce financial impact on motorists while maintaining the LEZ framework.
questions.sourceTitle: LEZ: maintained with annual pass and reduced fines

questions.question: What legal concern did the Council of State raise about reducing LEZ fines?
options: That lower fines violate a constitutional principle protecting public health and may be ineffective as a deterrent
That fines should only apply to commercial vehicles, not private car owners
That Brussels lacks authority to set fines without approval from the national government
That environmental monitoring technology is insufficiently advanced to justify fines
questions.explanation: The Council of State invoked the 'standstill principle'—a constitutional requirement that air quality protections cannot be weakened—and argued that the proposed reduced fines would be too low to effectively deter polluting vehicle use or protect residents' health. This precedent stems from a prior Constitutional Court ruling.
questions.sourceTitle: LEZ: maintained with annual pass and reduced fines

questions.question: According to the February 2026 government agreement, what replaces the frozen Metro 3 project as Brussels' major public transport investment?
options: A bus rapid transit system connecting the city center
A tram completing the loop of the existing network
An extension of the pre-metro system northward
A commuter rail line to surrounding municipalities
questions.explanation: The agreement explicitly replaces the Metro 3 underground extension with a surface tram that completes the existing tram network loop. This represents a shift from underground to above-ground public transport infrastructure.
questions.sourceTitle: Metro 3: project frozen for 10 years, replaced by tram

questions.question: What institutional body's report on cost overruns directly led negotiators to freeze the Metro 3 project?
options: The Brussels Parliament's transport committee
STIB, the public transport operator
The Court of Audit
The Beliris cooperation agreement secretariat
questions.explanation: The Court of Audit's critical report highlighting severe cost inflation and questioning the project's financial sustainability was the direct trigger for freezing Metro 3. This demonstrates how fiscal oversight institutions shape major infrastructure decisions in Brussels governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Metro 3: project frozen for 10 years, replaced by tram

questions.question: According to the Brussels government decree of July 2023, what is the maximum speed limit for electric e-scooters in pedestrian zones?
options: 5 km/h
8 km/h
15 km/h
20 km/h
questions.explanation: The regulatory framework distinguishes between general areas (20 km/h) and pedestrian zones (8 km/h) to enhance safety in high-traffic areas. This speed differentiation is a key mechanism for balancing micro-mobility accessibility with public safety.
questions.sourceTitle: Shared mobility in Brussels: e-scooters, bicycles, taxis and the battle over concessions

questions.question: What is the current status of the Villo! bicycle concession agreement with JCDecaux?
options: It was renewed for another 10 years in 2024
It expires in September 2026 after nearly 17 years
It has already been terminated as of 2025
It is under renegotiation with no fixed end date
questions.explanation: The original concession signed in November 2008 will end in September 2026, requiring JCDecaux to remove all stations within 7 months. The regional government launched a comparative study in 2023 to evaluate future scenarios, but no call for tenders has yet been announced.
questions.sourceTitle: Shared mobility in Brussels: e-scooters, bicycles, taxis and the battle over concessions

questions.question: In Brussels, which authority is responsible for approving and funding Dutch-language nurseries?
options: ONE (Office de la Naissance et de l'Enfance)
Opgroeien (formerly Kind en Gezin)
Iriscare
The Brussels municipalities directly
questions.explanation: Opgroeien, representing the Flemish Community, handles Dutch-language nursery approvals and funding in Brussels, while ONE manages francophone facilities. This split reflects Belgium's linguistic division of responsibilities.
questions.sourceTitle: Early childhood in Brussels: nurseries, waiting lists and triple institutional authority

questions.question: What is a key consequence of Brussels having three separate authorities managing nurseries instead of one regional authority?
options: Parents can choose between identical waiting lists across all nurseries
Parents must register separately on different waiting lists depending on the nursery type, which can cause months-long delays even when places are available elsewhere
The Brussels Region directly controls all nursery budgets and service quality
All families experience the same access to nursery places regardless of their commune
questions.explanation: Because ONE, Opgroeien, and Iriscare operate independently with separate waiting lists and budgets, families cannot access places across all systems simultaneously. The dossier illustrates how this fragmented structure creates inefficiency and inequality across Brussels.
questions.sourceTitle: Early childhood in Brussels: nurseries, waiting lists and triple institutional authority

questions.question: What is the primary source of PFAS contamination in the Brussels Region?
options: A PFAS production facility similar to the 3M factory in Flanders
Widespread use of PFAS-containing products and specific industrial sites
Agricultural runoff from surrounding regions
Exclusively from firefighting foam used by emergency services
questions.explanation: Unlike Flanders, Brussels has no major PFAS production facility. Contamination results from the widespread use of PFAS in consumer and industrial products, plus contamination from specific industrial sites like the former Sicli factory in Uccle.
questions.sourceTitle: PFAS in Brussels: soil and water contamination

questions.question: Which institution in Belgium has recommended significantly stricter health thresholds for PFAS in drinking water than current legal standards?
options: The European Environment Agency
Vivaqua water company management
The Superior Health Council
The Brussels Environmental Court
questions.explanation: The Superior Health Council (SHC) issued an opinion recommending much stricter thresholds based on the precautionary principle, distinguishing between 'maximum health values' and mere 'quality limits'—an important distinction not yet reflected in Belgian drinking water regulations.
questions.sourceTitle: PFAS in Brussels: soil and water contamination

questions.question: According to the Brussels security agreement of February 2026, how many separate police forces were operating at Gare du Midi before the new coordination plan?
options: 2 police forces
3 police forces
6 police forces
8 police forces
questions.explanation: The content identifies 6 local police zones plus the Federal Police, Federal Railway Police, SNCB security, and Stib guards as the multiple security actors at the station. This fragmentation was the key problem the new integrated coordination plan aims to solve.
questions.sourceTitle: Security plan: anti-drugs commissioner, 10M EUR for stations and police zone merger

questions.question: Under the new Brussels administration reform, which functions will be pooled together in the 'Shared support service' pillar?
options: Urban planning, housing, and employment services
Human resources, IT, finance, budget, and statistics
Public transport, parking, and environmental operations
Land management and real estate development
questions.explanation: Pillar 1 consolidates cross-cutting administrative functions that support all other pillars. Understanding this shared services model is key to grasping how the reform aims to reduce duplication and improve efficiency across Brussels' regional administration.
questions.sourceTitle: Reform of the regional administration: from 25 entities to 4 pillars

questions.question: Which two Brussels institutions jointly lead the Shifting Economy strategy, the Brussels-Capital Region's plan to redirect economic subsidies toward sustainable businesses?
options: Brussels Environment and Brussels Economy and Employment (BEE)
Brussels Environment and finance&invest.brussels
hub.brussels and Brupartners
Brussels Economy and Employment (BEE) and Brupartners
questions.explanation: Brussels Environment and BEE are the co-leaders of the Shifting Economy strategy, with support from hub.brussels for coaching and finance&invest.brussels for financial instruments. Understanding these institutional roles helps clarify how Brussels governance implements its economic transition plan.
questions.sourceTitle: Economic Transition: Shifting Economy, Labels and Sustainability Reporting

questions.question: What fundamental shift does the new accreditation standards reform for Brussels nursing homes (effective September 2024) represent?
options: A shift from a care-centred model to a resident-centred model focusing on autonomy, participation and quality of life
A shift from public facility management to mandatory private sector operation
A shift from individual care plans to standardized treatment protocols
A shift from family involvement to exclusive professional caregiver responsibility
questions.explanation: The new decree explicitly marks a transition toward prioritizing resident autonomy and participation rather than just service delivery. This represents a significant change in how elderly care facilities in Brussels approach their mission and can be explored further in the governance reform section.
questions.sourceTitle: Seniors in Brussels: housing, autonomy and data

questions.question: What was the main reason the SLRB's multi-year renovation plan and construction projects were frozen between June 2024 and February 2026?
options: A caretaker government was in place and could not approve new structural investments
The SLRB lacked sufficient financial resources to proceed with any projects
An audit of social housing organizations revealed governance failures that halted all work
The Brussels Region prioritized other spending areas over housing development
questions.explanation: The caretaker period (a transitional government without full decision-making powers) blocked structural measures. Only after the new government agreement of 12 February 2026 could these frozen projects be unblocked and move forward.
questions.sourceTitle: SLRB: 1,000+ public housing units and financial consolidation

questions.question: How is the SLRB addressing its financial consolidation challenge through asset sales?
options: Selling housing units directly to tenants at reduced prices to generate immediate revenue
Selling land sites and approximately 200 social housing units to reduce debt to the Brussels Region
Leasing public land to private developers in exchange for long-term rental income
Transferring ownership of all SISP organizations to reduce administrative costs
questions.explanation: The government decided to sell the Ariane and Palais sites (land parcels) and put about 200 social housing units up for sale. These disposals aim to reduce the SLRB's debt to the Region, alongside maintaining a credit line to avoid cash flow problems.
questions.sourceTitle: SLRB: 1,000+ public housing units and financial consolidation

questions.question: Why do aircraft departing Brussels Airport frequently overfly densely populated neighbourhoods in the city centre and north?
options: Because the airport's three runways are deliberately oriented towards Brussels to maximize passenger capacity
Because prevailing south-westerly winds require take-offs on a runway oriented towards the city
Because Brussels residents have requested flights to follow the canal route for safety reasons
Because federal regulations prohibit flights from departing towards the countryside
questions.explanation: The structural geography of the airport means that south-westerly winds—which occur most of the time—force departures on runway 25R towards Brussels, creating a 40-year conflict between airport operations and residents' quality of life. Understanding this helps explain why noise exposure is unavoidable without major infrastructure changes.
questions.sourceTitle: Brussels Overflight: 40 Years of Conflict, 450,000 Affected Residents

questions.question: Under the government agreement of February 2026, what is the Regional government's stake in Vivaqua's capital after the recapitalisation?
options: A minority stake of less than 50%
An equal 50% stake with other shareholders
A majority stake of over 50%
Full ownership of 100%
questions.explanation: The Region takes a 49.99% stake, which is just below the 50% threshold and represents a minority position. This structure allows the government to influence major decisions while maintaining private sector involvement in Vivaqua's management.
questions.sourceTitle: Vivaqua: financial consolidation announced
